title: Two Sum
description: Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return indices of the two numbers such that they add up to target. You may assume each input has exactly one solution, and you may not use the same element twice.
hints: Think about what information you need to find the complement.
For each number x, you need to find target - x.
A hash map lets you look up values in O(1).
approach: Use a hash map to store each number and its index as we iterate. For each element, check if its complement (target - num) already exists in the map.

title: Valid Parentheses
description: Given a string s containing just the characters '(', ')', '{', '}', '[' and ']', determine if the input string is valid. An input string is valid if open brackets are closed by the same type of brackets in the correct order.
hints: Use a stack to track opening brackets.
When you see a closing bracket, check if it matches the top of the stack.
At the end, the stack should be empty.
approach: Push opening brackets onto a stack. When a closing bracket is encountered, pop from the stack and verify it matches. If stack is empty at the end, the string is valid.

title: Merge Two Sorted Lists
description: You are given the heads of two sorted linked lists list1 and list2. Merge the two lists into one sorted list and return the head of the merged linked list.
hints: Use a dummy head node to simplify edge cases.
Compare the current nodes of both lists and attach the smaller one.
After one list is exhausted, attach the remainder of the other.
approach: Use a dummy head node. Compare the two list heads, attach the smaller node to the result, and advance that list's pointer. Append remaining nodes once one list is exhausted.

title: Binary Search
description: Given an array of integers nums which is sorted in ascending order, and an integer target, write a function to search target in nums. If target exists, return its index. Otherwise, return -1. You must write an algorithm with O(log n) runtime complexity.
hints: Maintain a left and right pointer.
Calculate mid = (left + right) // 2.
If nums[mid] == target, return mid. If smaller, move left. If larger, move right.
approach: Classic binary search. Keep halving the search space by comparing the middle element with the target.

title: Maximum Subarray
description: Given an integer array nums, find the subarray with the largest sum, and return its sum. A subarray is a contiguous non-empty sequence of elements within an array.
hints: Kadane's algorithm: track current sum and global max.
If current sum goes negative, reset it to 0 (start fresh).
At each step: currentSum = max(num, currentSum + num)
approach: Kadane's Algorithm: iterate through the array keeping track of the current running sum. If adding the current element makes things worse than starting fresh, reset. Track the global maximum throughout.

title: Reverse Linked List
description: Given the head of a singly linked list, reverse the list, and return the reversed list.
hints: You need three pointers: prev, curr, next.
At each step, save next, flip the arrow, then advance all three.
When curr is None, prev is the new head.
approach: Iterative approach using three pointers. At each step, reverse the next pointer of the current node, then advance all pointers forward.

solutions.python:
def reverseList(head):
    prev, curr = None, head
    while curr:
        nxt = curr.next
        curr.next = prev
        prev = curr
        curr = nxt
    return prev

title: Binary Tree Level Order Traversal
description: Given the root of a binary tree, return the level order traversal of its nodes' values (i.e., from left to right, level by level).
hints: Use a queue (BFS) — process nodes level by level.
At the start of each level, the queue size = number of nodes in that level.
Process exactly queue.size() nodes, then move to the next level.
approach: BFS using a queue. At each iteration, process all nodes at the current level (queue size at start of iteration), add their children to the queue, and record the level's values.

title: Number of Islands
description: Given an m x n 2D binary grid which represents a map of '1's (land) and '0's (water), return the number of islands. An island is surrounded by water and is formed by connecting adjacent lands horizontally or vertically.
hints: Iterate through each cell. When you find a '1', it's a new island.
DFS/BFS from that cell to mark all connected '1's as visited.
Increment island count each time you start a new DFS.
approach: Iterate through the grid. When a '1' is found, increment the count and DFS to mark all connected land cells as '0' (visited). This avoids double counting.

title: Number of 1 Bits
description: Given a positive integer n, write a function that returns the number of set bits in its binary representation (also known as the Hamming weight).
hints: n & (n-1) clears the lowest set bit each time.
Count how many times you can do this before n becomes 0.
Built-ins: bin(n).count('1') in Python, bits.OnesCount32 in Go, Integer.bitCount in Java.
approach: Use n & (n-1) to clear the lowest set bit each iteration. Count iterations until n reaches 0. This is O(k) where k is the number of set bits — faster than checking all 32 bits.

solutions.python:
def hammingWeight(n: int) -> int:
    count = 0
    while n:
        n &= n - 1  # clears lowest set bit
        count += 1
    return count

# One-liner
# return bin(n).count('1')

title: Counting Bits
description: Given an integer n, return an array ans of length n + 1 such that for each i (0 <= i <= n), ans[i] is the number of 1's in the binary representation of i.
hints: i >> 1 is i with the last bit removed — you already computed its count.
The last bit (i & 1) is either 0 or 1, just add it.
approach: DP: for each i, shift right by 1 (drop last bit) — already computed — then add the last bit (i & 1). This builds the answer in O(n) using prior results.

title: Reverse Bits
description: Reverse bits of a given 32-bit unsigned integer.
hints: Extract the last bit of n with (n & 1), put it into result.
Shift result left and n right by 1 each iteration.
Repeat 32 times — one per bit.
approach: Loop 32 times. Each iteration: shift result left by 1, OR in the lowest bit of n (n & 1), then shift n right by 1. After 32 iterations result holds the reversed bits.

title: Missing Number
description: Given an array nums containing n distinct numbers in the range [0, n], return the only number in the range that is missing from the array.
hints: Math: expected sum = n*(n+1)/2. Answer = expected - actual sum.
XOR: XOR all indices 0..n and all values. Paired values cancel out, leaving the missing one.
Math approach is simpler and O(1) space.
approach: Gauss formula: expected sum of [0..n] is n*(n+1)/2. Subtract the actual array sum to get the missing number. XOR alternative: XOR all indices and all values — everything cancels except the missing index.

title: Sum of Two Integers
description: Given two integers a and b, return the sum of the two integers without using the operators + and -.
hints: XOR (a ^ b) gives the sum bits without carrying.
AND shifted left ((a & b) << 1) gives the carry bits.
Repeat until there is no carry (b == 0).
approach: XOR gives sum without carry; AND<<1 gives the carry. Add carry into the partial sum by repeating until carry is 0. In Python, mask to 32 bits to handle Python's arbitrary-precision integers.

title: Rotate Image
description: You are given an n x n 2D matrix representing an image. Rotate the image by 90 degrees clockwise in-place.
hints: 90° clockwise = transpose then reverse each row.
Transpose: swap matrix[i][j] with matrix[j][i] for j > i.
Then reverse each row in-place.
approach: Two-step in-place: (1) Transpose the matrix by swapping matrix[i][j] with matrix[j][i]. (2) Reverse each row. This is equivalent to a 90° clockwise rotation.

title: Spiral Matrix
description: Given an m x n matrix, return all elements of the matrix in spiral order.
hints: Use four boundaries: top, bottom, left, right.
Traverse: right along top, down along right, left along bottom, up along left. Then shrink boundaries.
Check top <= bottom and left <= right before each pass to avoid duplicates.
approach: Maintain four boundary pointers (top, bottom, left, right). Traverse each layer: go right → down → left → up, then shrink all four boundaries inward. Guard the left/up passes to avoid re-visiting cells in non-square matrices.

title: Set Matrix Zeroes
description: Given an m x n integer matrix, if an element is 0, set its entire row and column to 0's. You must do it in place.
hints: First pass: record which rows and columns contain a zero.
Second pass: zero out those rows and columns.
O(1) space trick: use the first row and first column as your markers.
approach: Two-pass approach: first collect all (row, col) positions of zeros into sets. Second pass: if row or col is in the sets, set to 0. O(1) space upgrade: use matrix[0][c] and matrix[r][0] as flags, being careful to handle the first row/column separately.

title: Insert Interval
description: Given an array of non-overlapping intervals sorted by start time and a newInterval, insert the interval and merge if necessary. Return the resulting array.
hints: Add all intervals ending before newInterval starts
Merge all overlapping intervals
Add remaining intervals after newInterval ends
approach: Iterate through. Add intervals that end before new one starts. Merge all overlapping by expanding newInterval. Add the rest.

title: Merge Intervals
description: Given an array of intervals, merge all overlapping intervals and return an array of the non-overlapping intervals.
hints: Sort intervals by start time first
Compare each interval's start with the last merged interval's end
Merge if overlapping, else add new interval
approach: Sort by start. Iterate and merge if current start <= last merged end, otherwise append.

title: Non-overlapping Intervals
description: Given an array of intervals, return the minimum number of intervals you need to remove to make the rest of the intervals non-overlapping.

examples.explanation: Remove [1,3] and the rest are non-overlapping

hints: Sort by end time, not start time
Greedy: always keep the interval that ends earliest
Count intervals that can't be kept (they overlap with previous)
approach: Greedy: sort by end time. Keep an interval if its start >= prevEnd. Count removals otherwise.

title: Meeting Rooms
description: Given an array of meeting time intervals where intervals[i] = [start_i, end_i], determine if a person could attend all meetings.
hints: Sort intervals by start time
Check if any meeting starts before the previous one ends
One overlap means false
approach: Sort by start. If any interval starts before the previous ends, return false.

title: Meeting Rooms II
description: Given an array of meeting time intervals, return the minimum number of conference rooms required.
hints: Use a min-heap to track end times of ongoing meetings
Sort by start time
If earliest ending meeting ends before next starts, reuse that room
Heap size at end = number of rooms needed
approach: Sort by start. Use min-heap of end times. If heap top <= current start, pop (reuse room). Push current end. Heap size = rooms needed.
